speaker: interviewer
text: December 10, 1995. I'm interviewing Anita, A-N-I-T-A, Karl, K-A-R-L. My name is Ann, A-N-N, Page, P-A-G-E. We are in North Miami Beach, Florida, United States of America. We are speaking English. The date is December 10, 1995. I'm interviewing Anita Karl. My name is Ann Page. We are in North Miami Beach, Florida, United States of America. We are speaking English. This is the beginning of tape 1. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Anita Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-N-I-T-A. And my last name is spelled K-A-R-L, Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth, it was Ita, Ita Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-T-A, Karl, K-A-R-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you use any other names? Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: My nickname was Dzunia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-Z-U-N-I-A. And all my family at childhood used to call me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is May 26, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 57 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city and country were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lwow in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-W-O-W, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Samuel Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was Malca Brandt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sisters. One is younger, and one is older. The older one is [? Felice ?] Grubair Karl, and the youngest is Esther Oxenstein Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what business was your father in in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's business was in the textiles. They had a store, and he worked with his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And so you-- you did know your father's parents then? He worked with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names, your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparent's name was Fela, and Mark was her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Well, could you tell me some memories you have of your father from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood years were spent in a very lovely environment. All our families were alive on my father's side and on my mother's side. We were a very large family. On my mother's side, they were in the children carriages and toys manufacturing. They manufactured baby carriages. And my grandmother worked with her sons. She had five sons and five daughters. And the daughters took care of the rest of the family, the home, and the servants. And on my father's side, they were six brothers. All of them were married, and all of them had children, except for the elder one who had asked for permission from his father to become a doctor and was sent to study medicine in Vienna. As they were from a very Orthodox family and background, my grandfather was concerned that studying medicine will force him to break the Shabbat and other Jewish traditions. But when my uncle explained to him that he would do it for the good of humanity, then my grandfather Mark agreed and gave him a written permission, as it was used to do in those years. And so Joel Karl, who was the eldest of the brothers, went to Vienna and began his study in medicine. In later years, he became a very famous doctor and was director of a sanatorium in Vienna. But in 1938, he was told that because he was a Jew he was not allowed to practice medicine anymore and was asked to leave the country. The only country that gave him a visa, because he did apply to several ones, including the United States and other places, England, where everybody refused him, but Peru gave him a visa. And with a friend, they left in 1938 Vienna and traveled to Lima, where he established himself as a doctor. On the other brothers and sisters, in 1938 all the family was well, prosperous. They each had work. They each belonged to different societies. And although they heard about this things going on in Germany, it was so remote from us that nobody was afraid and didn't really know what was happening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Towards the end of 1938, the Russians came in, and the state would ask for a little while. But the Germans had declared war on Poland in 1939, and so the Russians left, and the Germans were approaching slowly but surely. And after the invasion of Poland, it took them two years to reach us. But when they came in into Lemberg, they came with a fierce hand, and all the persecutions and the nightmares began.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we go into that, could I ask you just a few more questions about your home life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you also remember your mother's parents, your grandparents on your mother's side? And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- the grandparents on-- on my mother's side were-- her name was Fela, and he was-- I don't remember his name right now. It just escaped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. But they were living close to you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were living close to us. No, her name was Etti. I am sorry. My grandmother's name was Etti, because my sister has her name. When my sister was born, she died. She died just as the Germans came in. She was one of the first ones to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was murdered on the street while she was going on her-- on-- on-- to her business. She was one of the first ones to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shot? Was she shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe she was shot, yes. She had those 10 children. They were a very, very loving family, and we all grew up together. Not living too far away from each other, we were able to see the families almost on a daily basis. We lived in a building that belonged to the family. And we had a nice apartment filled with all the latest commodities of that time, including lots of crystal and silver things. And being that we were kosher, so there were two sets of dishes for everything. And my mother and her sisters traveled a lot also to resort places, where they spent winters or summers with the family together to the countryside, where they had little homes. So life was very peaceful and very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you travel with them when they went away? Away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They took us with them, of course, us little children. And sometimes we were to the mountains, sometimes we were at the seashore. So it was a very nice and prosperous life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I realize you were very young. Did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no. And during the Russians-- while the Russians were with us, there was none. The Russians were more interested in people that had large amount of monies, and those they would take with them. And they were the lucky ones because they survived in Russia. The ones that were middle class, of course, stayed. And they did not bother anybody at all. The anti-Semitism in Poland-- Polish people and the Ukrainians that surrounded us were strongly anti-Semitic. But Lwow was a very progressive city, and it had a large Jewish community. So the Poles were, although envious of many, they would leave us alone. They did not bother us. The community was well established. There were several hundred, maybe 100,000 Jews. The city was one of the most elegant cities of Poland. It had an opera house. It had a museum. It had theaters. It had a Jewish theater that was world-renowned. The best artists from the United States would come there and perform. And from Poland, from Lwow to the United States, many came, and they were very, very well-known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had orchestras, symphonies, concerts, beautiful parks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. You remember those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that I remember we would go and play in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Would you tell me about your mother, what you remember about your mother as a small child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very, very loving woman dedicated to her husband and her children. We were three little girls. We had a brother. When they got married, they had a little boy first. By the age of three, he died of meningitis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a terrible blow to them, because being that he was a boy and a child that they gave so much love to. But in those years, there were no antibiotics, and he died shortly after he was three years old. And his name was Emmanuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, my mother was quite sad but recuperated and had the three of us, first my sister [? Felice ?]. Then a year and a half later, I was born. And three years later, my sister Esther was born. Our home was nice. We-- we were surrounded by all our aunts, who adored us, and our uncles. My mother was the youngest of ten-- five brothers and five sisters, very, very close-knit family. And they enjoyed their life to the fullest, helped out around the house. And my mother was-- wanted to study. So she did take classes at the conservatorium and became a concert pianist. At the conservatorium, they had religion, and she, as a young girl, refused because she was Jewish. So she didn't want to go to the Catholic classes. But her teacher always told her, Malca, stay, listen. You might learn something. You never know when it might come in handy. So she did that. And-- and she enjoyed painting and embroidery. She liked to do things with their hands. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you had a very happy home life, and you kept kosher. You remember the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this synagogue? Did it have a name or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called, I believe, the Great Synagogue of Lwow, which we attended of-- on all the holidays. And of course, Friday nights we had at home the Shabbat services with the family. And although my mother was a little bit more liberal, my father was very strict when it came to kashrut and orthodoxy. And my mother went to the mikvah every Friday night. And he insisted that she would do all those things, and she did. And she did, and we had a very Orthodox home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe to me the way you remember a Shabbat evening in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Shabbat evening was a very special occasion. Things were prepared a day before. My mother was a very good cook and baked beautiful and delicious things, and she prepared very nicely. Then candles were lit and songs were sung. And we were all dress up in special clothes. And we prepared when my father came from the synagogue. We would do the Kiddush and the prayers and have a traditional meal, a Shabbat meal that had the matzah [? kneydl ?] soup, the gefilte fish. And of course, in those times everything was made from scratch. You could not find anything packed or-- or in a jar. People would do them. And those times were nice and beautiful, and we looked forward to them very much so. And my little-- my older sister, of course, was always there with us. And she contributed also quite a bit to these special ceremonies and understood very well. We were trained from a-- a very early age to differentiate the [? milchig, ?] the milk things, and the kosher-- and the meat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Meat.

speaker: interviewee
text: --cutlery, and we were not allowed to mix them. And we knew perfectly well what everything meant, all this. And we were educated in a religious way, so we were geared to being an Orthodox Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Could you describe to me the events that happened precisely after the birth of your youngest sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1941, at the beginning of September, the Germans invaded Lwow. They came in, and they set up a command post. They were not many. And they started to interrogate all the people. They selected a few. Some of them-- them were Jewish. Some of them were not. And they found, surprisingly, a strong collaboration in the Ukrainian people, who would, without any fear, and the Polish people, point out who the Jews were, where they lived. And upon extracting a promise from the Germans that once the Jews would not be there any longer they would be able to take over their beautiful homes, they would point out to the Germans who they were, what were their names. But still, we were a big number. So it was not an easy deed to suddenly surround us all and carter us off someplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1941, my sister was born. She was born on September 15. And by that time, the Germans had already taken over a very old part of the town and decided that that would be the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto is a place where they put a certain type of people to live in misery and poverty. And in this way, they control what they do, if they go out, if they go in. They can number them. They were able to put names to each person and identify them to their use and to their advantage. So when my-- the ghetto was opened at the end of August, and slowly they were carting-- sending everybody else there. The day my sister was born-- and as it was customary in those times, the better class of people had their babies at home. They would not go to a hospital. And my sister-- my mother delivered my sister in her bed in our house. And a few hours later, there was a knock on the door. And the Germans appeared and told us that we had 24 hours to leave and go to the ghetto. My mother was distraught, because she was supposed to stay in bed at least a month. And they would not give her-- they would deny her that time. They-- they wanted her to go right away, but my father begged them to have mercy. And instead of just shooting us on the spot, somehow they decided that 24 hours would be-- would be appropriate. And in the meantime, they proceeded to devastate the whole house. They took, in front of us-- we were hiding at the bedside with-- around our mother. And they took all the linens, all the fine embroideries, all the silver, all the porcelain, and everything-- and every single item that they considered was an art object or was of any value, leaving us practically with none of the souvenirs or property that we-- mother had accumulated through the years. This taught us in later years to just use everything we have, to not put it away for a holiday or just for a special occasion. This was the first lesson that we learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that moment, my-- I was three. My sister was a year-- a month, and-- or not even a month, a day old. And the other one was four and a half. We found-- the next day, my father went to look for a place in the ghetto. Everything was terribly crowded. There were no apartments to be had. But a sister of my mother who had gone there, who had been told to go there a little bit earlier a few months before, she made space for us in her one-bedroom apartment. And so we were there-- 14 people, four families in a one-bedroom apartment. And the apartments were rather small. Remember, this was not the elegant part of the city. This was a very rundown part of the city. So the conveniences were inexistant. There was one tiny bathroom at the end of the corridor. And at the other end of the corridor, there were people already sleeping on mattresses on the floors. So we made do the best we could. At least we were together. They had also initiated the forced labor. They didn't want the people to get organized. So every morning, all the men, [? enabled ?] people and the young able boys, were taken out of the ghetto and were sent on different jobs and on different forced labors. There once a camp outside of Lemberg called Janowska, which was one of the most terrible extermination camps, where people died of illness, malnutrition, and just for sport for the Germans, who practiced on them their shooting skills. Life in the ghetto was very, very miserable. At the beginning, we were able to go in and out freely, but we had a curfew. At 7 o'clock, we must be back. And we were made to wear the Star of David on our chest. This was a yellow Star of David that everybody, under pain of death, was supposed to wear, children and adults alike.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in big pain, in the tremendous pain, my mother had to sew the Star on-- of David on all our clothes. But my father said-- he was such a gentle soul-- he told her, you are not sewing that in shame. You are sewing it in pride, because we are Jews and never forget this. This is not going to shame us. This is going to make us better. We will show them that we are human beings with pride. Did your parents bring anything with them into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were allowed to only bring one small suitcase with clothes. And of course, they chose to bring only for the children winter stuff, because the winter is very cruel in that part of Poland. And so we had sweaters and underwear, one pair of shoes maybe each, very little, because only one suitcase was allowed, and that's all. And old memorabilia, the family memories were left behind. My mother took the jewelry, which the Germans had not found, and so did all her sisters. Whoever was able to find some jewelry took it with them hidden in their undergarments, because we knew that this would be the only way we could survive. Barter had become the common way of getting to-- to food. Money had absolutely no worth. In the ghettos, they gave Juden money to the people. And you were only allowed to use that specific money that is only for the Jews. Then during the day, if a Jewish family left the ghetto to walk, wander on the streets, and get away from that, we were not allowed to visit the parks. The Jews were not allowed to walk into the beautiful parks that were the pride of the city. And there was a castle on a hill that belonged to a family Potocki. And when my mother wanted to enter with the baby carriage, the [INAUDIBLE] nurse from the-- the servants of the Graf Potocki would refuse us entrance because we were Jews. It was not allowed to show up in the stores that sold butter, sugar, milk, or bread, white bread. Those foods were not the staple of Jews.
